titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 11 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfred Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/54

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D1633/1/1/54 Nov 4 . 15 My I don't know whether you had better get
a daily letter from now on, however perhaps I'll chance
it if there is nothing to prevent my writing. Today I was tied to the office all day as Richardson
was doing an intelligence investigation but got out after dark for
a smart walk which I hope gave my liver a little
chance, but I really miss the cooking of our cooks
—excluding one I think- or is it the housekeeping of
a funny little creature which I really want, I wonder. This part of my anatomy misses the p r at
any rate still & perhaps then an other odd bits
inside which do also strange to say. I I wish am could get how alone every day
to sit down & write, but that is impossible so my letters
are scrappy. 11th Nov. Your news came last night about Rothamps.
Ted is wildly exciting. Brown Wolf is dreadfully alarmed
best he finds on his return a PR in a top hat with
big bone spectacles, sitting in a laboratory labelled on the door
Dr Lilian...? Silence No admission except to Germs
on duty; & a monster official outside warning off all
wolves & other persons with the caution Her Doctorship
is not to be disturbed. I wonder what you will decide
& if you go whether you will live in town or take
a house then! The journey to & thro' in the winter
does not inspire me especially the part from the King's +
to your flat in ? . It is lucky you haven't taken one
yet. I wonder what Lady Carson will say if you
tell her you have taken up the mantel of a
DSC LPRS DO DOPR. You won't guess
the meaning of these. I can hardly wait for this
morning's post to know what you are doing but suppose
in any case I shall not hear until another week at
any rate. Not much news. Gus' will probably be in
bad temper today having been sent for by Corps Com∨.
He does not like being a subordinate & like many
hectoring people resents anything in the way of orders
received. I am afraid our Divisional exercises have been
stopped because he is unwilling that the new Army
Com∨ should come & criticise. It reminds me of my
old difficulties & I wonder if Russell protests as I
should. I hope to get out this afternoon for a scamper, but
the weather reports which is rather good foretell a
rainy afternoon & it is bright sun now.
11 Nov I shall want to know full particulars of your visit
to Miss Blunt & who else was there; & what No
11 is doing of course. If a b w can get rid of a bad cold in 2 days
without help, will then p r be convinced that
cinnamons & tableltoids & suckies etc are
are not cures for us & and let him off in future. Probably after this he will have a cold for a month
at least! It is curious that the Divs in the trenches do
not wish to exchange with us for a rest behind,
even tho' winter is on. I admit I should be glad
to do so in their place. as regards the contrary I cannot
make up my mind. The experience would be very valuable,
& would insure our not young elsewhere, but trench life is
in many respects not good for the men who pick up a mode
of life which is not altogether good training for more active
work later. Best of love from your bw Wilfrid BS
